# Organic-First Campaign Plan — Central Europe Climate NGO

---

## 0. Assumptions

This brief was provided in full (Mode A). All 8 fields were explicitly stated. No defaults were applied. The table below surfaces key interpretive choices made during execution so they can be confirmed.

| Field | Value from brief | Interpretive choice |
|---|---|---|
| Sector | Environmental NGO, policy advocacy | Applying Rider 3 (Nonprofit / NGO / Cause-Led) |
| Outcome | 5,000 verified supporters (donation or signed petition) in 4 months | "Verified" treated as donation-confirmed or petition-with-email, not click |
| Audience | Climate-concerned adults 25–55, Hungary / Poland / Czechia / Slovakia, centre-left press readers, university graduates, not yet active donors | Multi-country: treated as parallel national sub-campaigns with shared narrative spine |
| Competition | Industry-front groups running slick Facebook ad campaigns; "affordable energy" / "energy sovereignty" frames; paid influencer relationships with 4 mid-tier creators | Names not provided in brief — described throughout at category level as "[opposition]" per anti-fabrication rule |
| Budget | €12,000 total / 4 months; board wants 40% (€4,800) on Facebook/Instagram; prior €4k paid failed | 40% paid earmark challenged below — see §5 |
| Existing channels | 8,000 email subscribers (12% open rate), 2,400 Instagram followers, 1 volunteer coordinator (Budapest + Krakow), partnership with 1 Direkt36-tier investigative outlet | Email list underperforming — 12% open rate signals disengagement, not just dormancy |
| Bottleneck | Trust — audience skeptical of NGOs after greenwashing scandals; believes the problem but not the messengers | Confirmed trust bottleneck → trust-compounding function in Stage 4 |
| Time window | 4 months to a hard policy vote deadline | Treated as 16 weeks: seed weeks 1–4, build weeks 5–10, mobilize weeks 11–16 |
| Capacity | 3 FTE campaigners + 2 comms staff at 50% = ~5 FTE equivalent; ~120h/week total stated | Conservative planning at 90h/week to account for meetings, admin, and unplanned events |

---

## 1. Campaign Ideas

Five concepts across distinct archetypes. All drawn from the brief's actual descriptors; [opposition] used throughout because the brief names the opposition only as a category, not by name.

---

### Concept A — "We Measured It Ourselves"
**Archetype:** Earned-Media Stunt + Counter-Narrative

**One-line thesis:** [Opposition] funds polished messaging; you fund rigorous citizen science — releasing verifiable local energy and emissions data from the four target countries before the vote makes their "affordable energy" frame look like what it is: marketing copy.

**Primary channel tier:** Tier 2 (earned media as spine) + Tier 1 (NGO staff and volunteer voices documenting the data collection).

**Authenticity hook:** Real measurement, real people in real towns doing it. The data cannot be manufactured; the methodology can be published; the opposition cannot replicate the local credibility. Specific sites, specific readings, specific dates.

**Minimum viable version:** One city (e.g., a specific industrial district in Budapest or Krakow where residents have documented air quality complaints). Two weeks of air-quality or energy-cost data. One co-published piece with the Direkt36-tier partner. Press package to three investigative reporters in target countries. If the data is newsworthy, the partner outlet leads; if it is not yet newsworthy, the pilot validates the methodology without burning resources.

---

### Concept B — "The People Who Pay for It"
**Archetype:** Founder-Story Arc (beneficiary as founder figure) + Mobilization

**One-line thesis:** Abstract climate statistics convert no one; one working family's documented energy bill, health cost, or flood-damage claim — told in their own voice, in their own language, in their own home — converts more than any polished ad.

**Primary channel tier:** Tier 1 (beneficiary-led social content) + Tier 2 (short-form video for Instagram Reels / TikTok, shared by volunteers into WhatsApp and private groups).

**Authenticity hook:** One named, consenting family in each country. They speak; the campaign listens and amplifies. The kitchen table, not the podium. The opposition's slick "energy sovereignty" ads have no counter for an actual person holding an actual bill.

**Minimum viable version:** One family, one country, one 90-second phone-filmed video. Volunteer shares it into three WhatsApp groups of professional peers. Count organic shares before deciding whether to produce more.

---

### Concept C — "What They Don't Want You to Read"
**Archetype:** Counter-Narrative + Coalition Play

**One-line thesis:** [Opposition]'s messaging frames are funded talking points — the NGO's partnership with investigative journalism and aligned research institutions can document who funds them and what the documented record says about their claims.

**Primary channel tier:** Tier 2 (investigative partner carries the story) + Tier 1 (NGO volunteers share into professional networks, Facebook Groups, university communities).

**Authenticity hook:** The investigative outlet is the credibility anchor, not the NGO. The NGO's role is to surface the story and provide the policy context; the journalist's byline does the trust work. The coalition of aligned orgs (academic, legal, consumer) co-signs the findings.

**Minimum viable version:** One published investigation co-produced with the existing Direkt36-tier partner. NGO provides policy context and data access; outlet provides editorial independence and reach. Measure petition signups attributed to the article.

---

### Concept D — "The 5,000 Neighbours"
**Archetype:** Movement Mobilization + Community-Build

**One-line thesis:** The campaign's 5,000-supporter goal is itself the campaign — a visible, growing roster of named professionals from the target countries who signed on before the vote, with peer-to-peer recruitment as the primary growth mechanic.

**Primary channel tier:** Tier 1 (volunteer coordinator networks, WhatsApp chains, chapter model in Budapest and Krakow) + Tier 2 (LinkedIn for the professional-audience segment, email list for direct conversion).

**Authenticity hook:** Real professional identities, listed by country and sector (doctors, teachers, engineers — not anonymous signatures). The list itself is the signal. Each new supporter sees the names of people like them who already signed. The opposition cannot manufacture 5,000 named, verified professionals.

**Minimum viable version:** Seed the list with 100 personally recruited supporters from existing volunteer and email networks (weeks 1–2). Launch the public counter at 100. Recruit from there.

---

### Concept E — "Absent from the Slick Campaign"
**Archetype:** Counter-Narrative + Cause-Led

**One-line thesis:** [Opposition] saturates Facebook with polished, well-funded ads; the NGO's deliberate absence from paid advertising — visible, named, explained — becomes the positioning: "We don't buy reach. We earn it."

**Primary channel tier:** Tier 1 (organic social from NGO staff accounts, volunteer networks) + Tier 2 (earned media on the contrast itself, organic Facebook Groups for community-of-practice audiences).

**Authenticity hook:** The refusal is the message. A public commitment: "We will not spend €4,800 on Facebook ads this campaign. Here is where those funds went instead: [specific community program, specific field research]." Make the budget decision visible and specific. The opposition cannot counter-position against transparency about money.

**Minimum viable version:** One post from the NGO director's personal account explaining the decision. One public budget transparency page showing where the €12,000 goes. Track organic shares vs. prior paid campaign performance.

---

## 2. Selected Concept

**Primary: Concept C — "What They Don't Want You to Read"** anchored to the investigative journalism partnership, as it directly addresses the trust bottleneck (third-party credibility) and leverages the NGO's most under-used asset (the Direkt36-tier partnership).

**Secondary layer: Concept D — "The 5,000 Neighbours"** provides the conversion mechanism. The investigation drives attention; the peer-recruitment mobilization converts attention into the 5,000 verified supporters.

**Supporting element from Concept B:** Beneficiary stories serve as the emotional spine across both concepts — the investigation names the systemic problem; the family stories make the cost felt.

The combined campaign is: investigative journalism drives earned media and trust; beneficiary stories provide the emotional conversion layer; peer-recruited professional supporters provide social proof and the 5,000 verified outcome.

---

## 3. Spend Asymmetry Verdict

Your asymmetry is **severe** (the [opposition] campaign's budget is roughly 30× yours). This means the organic-first playbook is mandatory, not optional — every paid euro must be justified by a prior organic proof point, the board's 40% Facebook earmark should be redirected (see §5), and your competitive advantage is authenticity and specificity, not reach.

**Preconditions Check (6 factors):**

1. **Credible insider / defector equivalent.** Partial. The NGO has staff with genuine expertise and the investigative journalism partner provides third-party credibility. A named executive director or researcher willing to speak personally would strengthen this significantly. Score: 0.5/1.

2. **Accumulated grievance.** Yes. Climate-concerned adults in these four countries have watched the gap between political promises and policy delivery widen over years. The audience believes the problem (per brief) — the grievance is present but attached to the wrong messenger (NGOs). Score: 1/1.

3. **Consolidated challenger field.** Partial. The brief does not name other NGOs in the coalition. If three other climate NGOs are running competing campaigns for the same petition signers, the field is fragmented and organic narrative dilutes. Building or joining a coalition (Concept C's coalition play) addresses this. Flag: this is the weakest precondition and should be a campaign sub-goal in weeks 1–2. Score: 0.5/1.

4. **Felt economic pain.** Yes. Energy costs, flooding, and air quality are directly experienced in all four target countries. The audience experiences the cost; it is not abstract. Score: 1/1.

5. **Threshold-rewarding system.** Yes. A policy vote with a specific date has binary outcome — the vote passes or it does not. This creates genuine urgency and a clear Now moment in the Ganz framework. Score: 1/1.

6. **Incumbent overplaying a fear / saturation hand.** Yes. [Opposition] is running slick paid-Facebook campaigns with polished "energy sovereignty" messaging. At 30× spend, saturation fatigue is likely in the target audience. The over-produced paid content is already an asset for counter-positioning. Score: 1/1.

**Score: 5/6.** The organic-first playbook is appropriate at full scale. Proceed to Stage 4. The one weak precondition — consolidated challenger field — is a campaign sub-goal: in weeks 1–2, identify and formally align with 2–3 other NGOs or research institutions operating in this space. An isolated campaign from one NGO amplifies the trust deficit; a named coalition of credible organizations dilutes it.

---

## 3a. Message-Market-Fit Gate

**Q1 — Revenue/commitment signal:** Has the NGO received ≥10 people signing petitions, donating, or volunteering without being directly prompted?
Answer from brief: The email list of 8,000 and the existing volunteer coordinator running regional meetups in Budapest and Krakow confirm ≥10 past committed supporters. **YES.**

**Q2 — Language signal:** Can the campaign name a specific pain the audience articulates in their own words before the pitch?
Answer from brief: "Affordable energy" and "energy sovereignty" are the [opposition]'s frames — the brief confirms the audience is aware of and pushes back against these frames. The 12% open rate on the email list suggests existing subscribers know the issue. The brief notes "they believe the problem but not the messengers." That is a specific articulated position. **YES.**

**Q3 — Close signal:** If 5 people from the target audience were asked to sign the petition today, would 3 say yes?
Answer inferred from brief: Prior paid campaigns converted poorly (€4k with no stated conversion numbers), but the bottleneck is trust in the messenger, not belief in the cause. The audience already believes the problem. With a trusted ask (peer referral, investigative outlet co-sign, named coalition), 3/5 commitment is plausible. Treating as conditional YES with a caveat: the first 2 weeks of earned-media work must validate this before broad mobilization.

**Verdict: 3/3 — MMF confirmed.** The bottleneck is distribution and trust in the messenger, not demand for the cause. The organic-first playbook is the right intervention. The campaign is a distribution problem, not an offer problem.

---

## 4. Channel Tier Stack

**Primary function: Trust compounding.** The brief explicitly names trust as the bottleneck. The audience believes the cause; they do not trust NGOs as messengers. Every channel choice routes through this function. Demand capture and paid amplification are secondary.

**Sector rider applied: Rider 3 — Nonprofit / NGO / Cause-Led.** Key adjustments: (a) earned media on specific named beneficiaries and documented findings leads over abstract cause messaging; (b) coalition play is an archetype default; (c) volunteer flywheel is the primary growth mechanic; (d) cold paid acquisition is off the table — donor LTV math does not work; (e) donor-speak is the primary failure mode to avoid (see §11).

**Multi-country note:** Four countries, one campaign spine, four adapted delivery mechanisms. The investigative journalism asset and the Ganz-structured beneficiary stories are country-agnostic. The petition/donation page, WhatsApp sharing networks, and earned-media targets need country-specific versions.

| Priority | Channel | Tier | Function | Weekly effort | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Investigative partnership — co-produced content with Direkt36-tier outlet | Tier 2 (earned media) | Trust compounding | 8h/week (NGO staff time on data provision, fact-check, liaison) | The outlet's editorial independence is the trust signal. NGO provides data; journalist provides credibility. |
| 2 | Email list — segmented, beneficiary-led narratives | Tier 1 | Trust compounding → conversion | 4h/week | Current 12% open rate signals disengaged list. Re-engagement sequence first (see §8). Segment by country for adapted sends. |
| 3 | Volunteer network — Budapest and Krakow chapters + peer WhatsApp chains | Tier 1 | Mobilization → conversion | 6h/week (coordinator + NGO staff) | Ground game: every volunteer is ~45× more persuasive per person reached than a paid impression. Expand to Prague and Bratislava by week 5. |
| 4 | Personal social accounts — NGO director and named staff/researchers | Tier 1 | Trust compounding | 4h/week | Brand accounts carry zero trust with a skeptical audience. Named individuals speaking as themselves do. Ganz Self/Us/Now structure on every post. |
| 5 | Coalition partner channels — aligned NGOs, academic institutions, legal bodies | Tier 1–2 | Trust compounding + reach extension | 2h/week (relationship management) | Each coalition partner sharing the investigative finding multiplies reach with a credibility endorsement. Prioritize partners whose audiences overlap target but who have not been tainted by greenwashing perception. |
| 6 | Organic Facebook Groups — regional environmental, professional, neighbourhood groups in target countries | Tier 2 | Reach + conversion | 3h/week | Facebook Pages are dead (0.15% organic engagement). Groups still work. Target groups where the audience already gathers without NGO prompting. |
| 7 | LinkedIn — NGO director and staff thought leadership | Tier 2 | Trust compounding (professional audience) | 2h/week | University graduates and professionals in the target demographic are reachable here. Specific, data-backed posts outperform advocacy posts. |
| 8 | Warm retargeting — email list and website visitors only | Tier 3 | Paid amplification of proven organic winners | 1h/week management; €1,500–2,000 total budget | Only after organic traction gate passed (§9). Warm list only — no cold audiences. |

**Channels explicitly not recommended:**

- **Cold Facebook/Instagram ads (Tier 4):** The board's 40% earmark (€4,800) should not go here. Prior €4k paid run failed. The audience is specifically skeptical of NGO advertising. Spending €4,800 on cold paid to an audience that distrusts NGO ads is not amplification — it is evidence to the audience that the NGO operates like the [opposition] they distrust. Redirect this budget (see §5).
- **Paid influencer relationships:** The [opposition] has four paid mid-tier creators. Copying this tactic positions the NGO alongside the opponent in the audience's mental model. The counter-position is earned credibility, not purchased reach.
- **Broad Google Ads:** No demonstrated search demand to capture for this specific policy campaign. Budget is better spent on earned media.

---

## 5. 70/30 (or 80/20) Allocation

**Recommended allocation: 90/10 organic/paid.**

Severe asymmetry at 1:30 ratio warrants 80/20 minimum (per skill rules). The trust bottleneck, the prior paid failure (€4k with poor conversion), and the [opposition]'s saturation of paid channels all push further toward organic. The 90/10 recommendation is conservative but defensible.

**Budget reallocation from the board's 40% earmark:**

The board has earmarked €4,800 for Facebook/Instagram ads. This should not be spent as planned. The reasons:

1. Prior €4k paid run converted poorly — this is direct evidence, not theory.
2. The trust bottleneck means the audience does not convert from ads, regardless of creative quality.
3. The [opposition] already saturates paid Facebook — adding NGO ads to that saturated feed increases the noise, not the signal.
4. Platform attribution will show ROAS; incremental lift (see §10) will not confirm it.

**Proposed reallocation of the €4,800:**

| Use | Amount | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| Investigative journalism data-gathering support (field costs, travel, equipment for citizen-science element if Concept A element is added) | €1,500 | This produces the content that drives earned media — the highest-trust, lowest-cost channel |
| Volunteer coordinator expansion — Prague and Bratislava on-the-ground recruitment and chapter setup | €1,000 | Ground game is the only channel that compounds into the 4-month window at this asymmetry level |
| Beneficiary story production — phone-quality video, travel to interview families | €800 | One named family per country, four films, produced authentically (not studio) |
| Warm retargeting of email list and past website visitors only, after organic traction gate passes | €1,500 | The one legitimate use of paid budget — retargeting a warm audience that already knows the NGO |
| Reserve for earned-media moment amplification (if a specific investigation or beneficiary story breaks into mainstream press, a small amplification budget can extend reach at that moment) | €1,000 | Hold until a specific organic winner exists — do not pre-commit |

**Total organic budget:** €12,000 - €1,500 (warm retargeting, conditional) = €10,500 toward organic and production.
**Effective organic/paid split:** ~88/12, with the 12% paid contingent on organic traction gate.

---

## 6. Competitor Saturation Map

Note: the brief names the opposition only as "industry-front groups" and "fossil-fuel-lobby-linked opposition." No specific organization names appear in the brief. The saturation map describes the opposition at category level as directed by the brief and the skill's anti-fabrication rule.

---

**[Opposition]: Industry-front groups running slick Facebook ad campaigns**

**(a) What they saturate:**
Paid-heavy Facebook and Instagram campaigns with high production value: polished graphics, professional voiceovers, stock-footage B-roll of working-class families and energy infrastructure. Message frames anchored to "affordable energy" and "energy sovereignty" — economic safety framing, not environmental framing. Paid influencer relationships with four named mid-tier creators (brief's description) amplifying the same frames. The content looks expensive because it is — and because the budget is visible in the production, it reads to a skeptical audience as: someone with money wants me to believe this.

**(b) The absence that becomes your signal:**
[Opposition] cannot produce: named local researchers publishing raw data. Named families speaking in their own language about their own energy bills. An investigative journalist's byline co-signing a finding. A coalition of universities and NGOs with documented methodology. The absence of verifiable provenance is the saturation strategy's structural weakness — paid production can look real; it cannot be real in the ways that matter to an audience that has been burned by greenwashing.

Your signal is the refusal to look like them. Every unpolished phone video, every published data methodology, every named local voice is counter-positioning by format, not just by message.

**(c) One-sentence positioning line:**
"We don't pay for your agreement — we show you the evidence and let you decide."

This line must hold across every piece of content: the investigative findings, the beneficiary stories, the petition ask, the volunteer recruitment, the email subject lines. When the campaign is tempted to use urgency language, fear framing, or polished advocacy copy — check it against this line.

---

## 7. Three Alternative Campaign Shapes

Each shape executes the selected concept (investigative journalism + peer mobilization, with beneficiary stories as the emotional layer). Each includes a flagship content piece structured as Self / Us / Now (Ganz).

---

### Shape A — Earned-Media First

**How it works:** The investigative partner publishes a major accountability finding in weeks 3–4. The campaign holds all other activity until that moment, then releases a coordinated wave: beneficiary stories, volunteer mobilization, petition ask, coalition co-signatures — all in the 72 hours following publication.

**Flagship content (Self / Us / Now):**
- *Self:* The NGO director writes a personal essay — why they entered climate advocacy, what they have seen change in the countries they work in, what they are afraid of if this vote does not go the right way. Specific. First person. Not organizational.
- *Us:* The investigative piece documents what the audiences in all four countries share: the energy cost increase over the past 5 years, the gap between the promises in [opposition] advertising and the documented record.
- *Now:* "The vote is in [X] weeks. We have published the evidence. If you believe it should reach the people making this decision, sign here and share this with one person you trust."

**Tradeoffs:**
- Pro: Concentrates the campaign's energy on one high-trust moment instead of drip-feeding low-trust posts. Press coverage of the investigation does more for NGO credibility than 4 months of social posts.
- Con: The entire campaign depends on the investigative piece being ready, newsworthy, and published on schedule. If the outlet delays, the campaign loses its spine. Risk mitigation: begin data gathering in week 1; confirm publication commitment by end of week 3.

---

### Shape B — Community-First

**How it works:** Build the "5,000 Neighbours" roster before any public communication. Weeks 1–4 are entirely internal: recruiting the first 200 named, verified professionals through direct outreach from volunteer coordinator and staff personal networks. Launch publicly at 200, with the names visible. Then open peer recruitment.

**Flagship content (Self / Us / Now):**
- *Self:* One of the first 200 supporters — a doctor, teacher, or engineer — writes a 300-word explanation of why they signed. Not the NGO's words; their words. Published on the campaign site and shared by the NGO director with attribution.
- *Us:* "Two hundred professionals in Hungary, Poland, Czechia, and Slovakia have already signed their name to this. They are teachers, doctors, engineers, and researchers who experience the cost of this policy directly. Look at the list. You probably know someone on it."
- *Now:* "The vote is in [X] weeks. Add your name. Share this with one colleague who hasn't signed yet."

**Tradeoffs:**
- Pro: The peer-recruitment mechanic is the highest-trust conversion path. A friend's name on a list converts better than any ad. The NGO's trust problem is bypassed when the ask comes from a known peer.
- Con: Slower start. Requires sustained volunteer coordinator effort in weeks 1–4 with no visible public activity. The board may pressure for visible output; manage expectations early.

---

### Shape C — Beneficiary-Story First

**How it works:** Lead with one beneficiary story per country, released weekly across weeks 1–4. Each story is a phone-filmed 90-second video from a named family, in their language, structured as Self / Us / Now. The investigative journalism element launches in week 5 as the systemic context for the personal stories. The petition opens in week 3.

**Flagship content (Self / Us / Now):**
- *Self:* One family, one country. "This is our energy bill from January. This is what we cut to pay it." Specific numbers. Real home. No production.
- *Us:* "Every family in this building faces the same choice. Every family in this city. Every family across [country]. The difference is: most of them don't know it's a policy decision, not a market one."
- *Now:* "The policy vote that will change this is in [X] weeks. We are collecting 5,000 names before it happens. If this looks like your family's situation, we need your name."

**Tradeoffs:**
- Pro: Highest emotional resonance. Beneficiary stories convert better than organizational claims at the individual decision point. Works well in WhatsApp and dark social — family members share with family members.
- Con: Requires finding and preparing families willing to be named and filmed. This takes time and care (consent, safety considerations, potential retaliation from [opposition]). If the families cannot be found in weeks 1–2, Shape C collapses. Begin recruitment immediately, in parallel with other shapes.

---

**Recommendation:** Run Shape A and Shape B in parallel with a shared beneficiary-story layer from Shape C. The investigative piece (Shape A) builds the institutional credibility argument; the named-professionals roster (Shape B) builds the social proof conversion path; the beneficiary stories (Shape C) provide the emotional layer that makes abstract policy felt. Each element feeds the others without requiring all three to succeed independently.

---

## 8. First-30-Days Action List

**Capacity baseline:** 90 usable hours/week across the team. Allocated below. All actions are owned by a role; if staff allocation changes, the cuts section at the bottom names which actions to drop first.

---

### Week 1 — Foundation

**Goal:** No public launches. Build the infrastructure that makes everything else possible.

| Action | Owner | Effort | Success signal |
|---|---|---|---|
| Draft and agree the one-sentence positioning line with the full team | NGO director + comms lead | 2h | Line is written, agreed, printed on the wall, and will appear on every piece of review output |
| Map coalition landscape: identify 3–5 organizations (academic, legal, consumer) willing to co-sign the investigative findings | Campaigner 1 | 8h | List of 5 potential coalition partners with named contact and a yes/maybe/no classification |
| Contact the Direkt36-tier partner outlet: confirm the co-investigation brief, agree on data-provision timeline, confirm publication window | NGO director | 3h | Written confirmation of joint brief, provisional publication date, editorial contact named |
| Begin beneficiary family recruitment in Hungary and Poland: identify community coordinators in target cities who can introduce the campaign to families | Volunteer coordinator | 6h | At least 3 families in conversation by end of week |
| Audit the email list: segment by country and by last-open date. Identify the engaged 20% (those who opened in the last 90 days) | Comms lead | 4h | Segmented list exported; engaged cohort identified |
| Set up UTM tracking for all campaign links: petition, donation, newsletter, investigative piece | Campaigner 2 | 3h | UTM scheme documented; all campaign URLs tagged before any public activity |
| Begin recruiting the first 50 named professionals for the "5,000 Neighbours" roster through direct outreach from personal networks | All staff — 5 contacts each | 5h (distributed) | 50 direct outreach messages sent; track responses |
| Begin data gathering for investigative element: energy cost documentation, emissions data from public sources in target countries | Campaigner 3 | 8h | Data sources catalogued, gaps identified, field measurement plan written |

**Week 1 total: ~39h. Within 90h capacity.**

---

### Week 2 — Infrastructure Complete, Beneficiary Stories Begin

| Action | Owner | Effort | Success signal |
|---|---|---|---|
| Film first beneficiary story (one family, in-country, phone camera) | Campaigner 1 + volunteer coordinator | 8h (travel + film + edit) | 90-second video in the can, family has reviewed and consented |
| Launch re-engagement email to engaged email segment: honest, personal, from the NGO director — "here's what we've been working on, here's why we haven't emailed more, here's what's at stake in the next 4 months" | Comms lead | 4h | 25%+ open rate on re-engagement send (vs. current 12% baseline); click rate >3% |
| Confirm coalition partners: secure written commitment from 2 organizations to co-sign findings | Campaigner 1 | 4h | 2 signed coalition letters |
| Begin NGO director personal social posting — 3 posts per week on LinkedIn and personal Facebook/Instagram, Ganz-structured, from named account (not brand account) | NGO director | 3h/week ongoing | Each post receives >5 substantive comments within 48h (not likes — comments) |
| Peer-to-peer professional roster: follow up on week-1 outreach; send next wave of 50 personal invites | All staff | 4h | Roster at 30–50 named, confirmed signatories by end of week 2 |
| Identify earned media targets in each country: journalists at independent outlets who cover energy policy and have recent relevant bylines | Campaigner 2 | 6h | List of 15 journalists across 4 countries with RSS feeds + contact channel + most recent relevant article |
| Identify 10 active Facebook Groups in target countries where the audience already gathers (regional environmental groups, professional associations, neighbourhood groups) | Campaigner 3 | 3h | List with group size, moderation contact, posting rules |

**Week 2 total: ~32h. Within capacity.**

---

### Week 3 — First Public Content; Petition Opens

| Action | Owner | Effort | Success signal |
|---|---|---|---|
| Release first beneficiary story — shared by NGO director personally, not from brand account. Post to director's personal social, share into 3 Facebook Groups (with group moderator pre-approval), share into volunteer WhatsApp chains for onward peer sharing | NGO director + comms lead | 3h | 50+ organic shares in 48h; 10+ substantive comments; petition opens and receives first 50 signups within 72h |
| Open petition and donation page — linked from beneficiary story | Campaigner 2 | 2h setup | Page live, UTM tracking confirmed, first signups logged |
| Send email to full list — not re-engagement tone, but the beneficiary story as content, with petition ask as the Now | Comms lead | 3h | 15%+ open rate; 2%+ click-to-petition conversion from email (target: 100+ signups from this send alone) |
| Pitch investigative finding preview to 3 journalists from the week-2 target list — not the full story, but the data angle and the policy hook | Campaigner 1 | 4h | At least 1 journalist expresses interest and requests full briefing |
| Second beneficiary story (second country) — film and edit | Campaigner 1 + volunteer | 8h | Story ready to release week 4 |
| Coalition partner LinkedIn and social posts — partners co-post the beneficiary story with their own endorsement | Campaigner 3 | 2h coordination | Each coalition partner posts; track reach via UTM |
| Check organic traction gate on beneficiary story: did it pass? (saves, shares, sustained watch time in first 48h) | Comms lead | 1h | Decision logged: boost or do not boost |

**Week 3 total: ~23h. Within capacity.**

---

### Week 4 — Earned Media Pitching; Roster Push

| Action | Owner | Effort | Success signal |
|---|---|---|---|
| Release second beneficiary story (second country) — same distribution playbook as week 3 | NGO director + comms lead | 3h | 50+ organic shares in 48h; 50+ additional petition signups |
| Full media pitch to 5 journalists: provide the full data package, the co-investigation brief summary, and a named source for each country | Campaigner 1 | 6h | At least 2 journalists commit to covering the investigation publication; 1 requests embargo access to findings |
| Roster at 200 — public launch of "5,000 Neighbours" counter | All staff | 2h | Counter goes live on campaign site; email send to full list announcing 200 named signatories; social posts from director's account |
| Launch WhatsApp sharing campaign: volunteer coordinator trains Budapest and Krakow chapter members to share petition into personal networks with the peer-ask script | Volunteer coordinator | 4h | Each chapter member sends to minimum 10 personal contacts; track resulting petition signups by UTM (referral-whatsapp) |
| Expand to Prague and Bratislava: recruit chapter leads in two new cities | Volunteer coordinator | 4h | Named chapter leads in Prague and Bratislava confirmed; first meetups scheduled |
| Monthly metric review: email open rates, petition signups by source, organic shares by content piece, roster growth | Comms lead | 2h | Dashboard with per-channel conversion rates; identify top channel for doubling effort in month 2 |

**Week 4 total: ~21h. Within capacity.**

---

**Community-build sub-campaign (runs in parallel across all 4 weeks):**

A WhatsApp coordination group for campaign volunteers is not optional — it is the mobilization infrastructure. The alternative (email-only coordination) has a 48h latency that will kill time-sensitive actions.

- **Before week 1 ends:** Volunteer coordinator creates volunteer WhatsApp groups by country. Writes group rules. Seeds with 10 personally invited activists from existing Budapest and Krakow networks.
- **Weeks 1–2:** Volunteer coordinator posts daily in each group — updates, content to share, asks. Non-negotiable. Dead groups are worse than no groups.
- **Weeks 3–4:** Hand off 3 recurring rituals to engaged early members: weekly "share this" thread, weekly sign-up count update, member-spotlight recognition. If no members step up by week 4, treat as a mobilization failure — investigate the ask and the friction, not the platform.
- **Month 2+:** Budget 3h/week sustained for coordination, moderation, new member welcomes.

---

**Capacity check and cuts:**

The week-by-week list above totals approximately 115h across 4 weeks (28.75h average per week), well within the 90h/week capacity. No cuts required at this capacity level.

If capacity drops (staff illness, other organizational priorities), cut in this order: (1) Facebook Group posting (Campaigner 3, week 2 onward — 3h/week), (2) LinkedIn posting (2h/week — maintain director's personal account posting but pause comms staff support), (3) second and third country beneficiary story production in weeks 3–4 (defer to month 2). Do not cut: investigative partnership liaison, email sends, or volunteer WhatsApp coordination — these three are the campaign's structural spine.

---

## 9. Ad Copy + Boost Rules

The board's 40% paid earmark has been redirected (§5). The remaining paid budget is €1,500 for warm retargeting and €1,000 in reserve for amplification of a proven organic winner.

**Paid is only triggered if two conditions are both met:**

1. **Organic traction gate:** A specific piece of content (beneficiary story, investigative piece, NGO director post) passes the 24–48h organic gate — saves, shares, sustained watch time, deep-thread comments. Likes alone do not qualify; they are cheap and often bot-inflated.

2. **Audience definition:** Warm audiences only. The email list (8,000 subscribers, segmented to the engaged 20% first), website custom audiences from the petition page, past social engagers. No cold audiences. No lookalike expansion until the warm audience has been exhausted and a lift test confirms incremental value.

**Creative direction (if boost is triggered):**

- The content that earned organic traction is the content that gets boosted. Do not produce new creative for paid — boost the real thing.
- No production upgrades. If the original was shot on a phone in someone's kitchen, the boosted version is also shot on a phone in someone's kitchen. Adding motion graphics or a voiceover to an organically successful piece signals that the original was a test, not real — it breaks the authenticity signal.
- Copy: first person, from a named individual (NGO director or a named volunteer), structured as Ganz Self / Us / Now. No organizational "we." No urgency language without a real deadline attached to a real event.
- CTA: single, specific. "Sign the petition by [date] before the policy vote." Not "learn more," not "support our work," not "join us." One action, named, with a deadline.

**Boost cap:**

- Frequency cap: 5–6 impressions per person. Beyond that, the content is annoying, not persuading. Set frequency cap before launching.
- Creative refresh: if the boost runs for more than 2 weeks, refresh the creative (a new post from the same person, same format) rather than extending the original. Ad fatigue in this political/advocacy category compresses faster than commercial — expect 10–12 days before fatigue sets in, not the traditional 4 weeks.

**What to refuse:**

- Do not bid on the NGO's own name keywords in search. There is no brand-keyword search volume that justifies this spend at this budget level, and the Blake/Nosko/Tadelis research on eBay (2015) shows brand-keyword search ad lift is often zero even for established brands — traffic arrives organically regardless.
- Do not run cold audience campaigns on Facebook or Instagram. The prior €4k run is direct evidence; the trust bottleneck is structural evidence; the [opposition]'s saturation of the same channel is competitive evidence. Three independent reasons to refuse.

---

## 10. Lift-Test / Measurement Plan

**Template 5 — Organic-source attribution (zero/minimal budget baseline)**

This campaign does not have meaningful paid spend to test against a holdout until the organic traction gate is passed and the warm retargeting is activated (conditional on organic success). Template 5 runs from day 1 for the organic campaign. If warm retargeting is activated, Template 1 (geo-holdout) supplements it.

**Template 5 — Organic source attribution:**

**Hypothesis:** Email newsletter converts at a higher rate per unique visitor than organic social media posts (LinkedIn, Instagram, Facebook Groups), because the existing email list has pre-existing NGO relationship even if that relationship is low-engagement; and investigative journalism referrals convert at a higher rate than either, because third-party editorial credibility bypasses the trust deficit.

**Design:**

- UTM-tag every link: `utm_source=<channel>` (newsletter, linkedin, facebook-groups, instagram, whatsapp, investigative-partner, coalition-partner-X), `utm_medium=organic`, `utm_campaign=climate-policy-2026`.
- Track conversions (petition signup, donation) by UTM source on the campaign landing page. Standard analytics (Plausible or PostHog) handles this natively.
- Run for 30 days minimum (covers weekly cyclicality; do not interpret week-1 data alone).
- Rank channels by conversion rate per unique visitor — not absolute conversion volume. A newsletter with 1,600 engaged readers (20% of 8,000) and 80 petition signups (5%) outperforms a Facebook Group post with 5,000 reach and 90 signups (1.8%).

**Decision rule:**
- Top channel by conversion rate → double weekly effort on it in month 2.
- Any channel below 20% of top channel's conversion rate → drop it from active effort unless it serves a secondary function (SEO backlink, coalition relationship maintenance, credibility signaling) the conversion metric cannot see.
- Middle band → maintain effort, retest after any format change.
- If overall conversion rate across all channels drops below 0.5% → re-run the MMF gate in §3a. The bottleneck has shifted upstream — the distribution is working but something in the offer (the ask level, the petition design, the CTA) is broken.

**Decision threshold for organic campaign success:**
- End of month 1: 500+ verified petition signatories with tracked UTM source. Anything below 200 by end of week 4 requires an immediate format review (is the ask too high? is the landing page converting? is the petition ask reaching the wrong audience segment?).
- End of month 2: 2,000+ signatories. Pace check against the 5,000 goal.
- End of month 4: 5,000 verified signatories.

---

**Template 1 supplement — Geo-holdout for warm retargeting (if activated):**

Condition: warm retargeting is activated after organic traction gate is passed.

- **Test region:** Hungary (Budapest and major cities — this is where the volunteer coordinator has established ground presence and the email list has highest density).
- **Holdout region:** Czechia (similar demographic profile in the target audience; no retargeting ads run here — organic only).
- **Duration:** 4 weeks.
- **Metric:** Petition signups per week (pulled from campaign analytics, segmented by country).
- **Decision threshold:** Continue retargeting spend only if Hungary signups are at least 1.8× the difference-in-differences delta vs. spend cost (i.e., if €750 of retargeting spend produces measurably more than €750 worth of incremental petition signups at the campaign's cost-per-verified-supporter rate). Stop if delta is within noise. Reallocate to organic if stopped.

---

## 11. Anti-Vanity Metric Dashboard

**Track these:**

| Metric | Why it matters | How to measure |
|---|---|---|
| Petition signups with verified email | The outcome. Unverified signups are noise. | Campaign landing page analytics by UTM source; email verification on submission |
| Donation count and average gift size | Directly counts toward the 5,000 verified supporter goal; also a conversion quality signal | Donation platform analytics |
| Email open rate by segment (engaged vs. lapsed vs. new) | Tracks whether the trust-rebuilding is working with the list | Email platform segmented reporting |
| Per-channel conversion rate (petition signups / unique visitors per UTM source) | Tells you which channel actually converts, not which one has the most impressions | Analytics platform, UTM-segmented |
| Named professionals on the roster by country | Social proof mechanic; the number visible on the site is a conversion trigger | Campaign site counter + manual verification |
| Volunteer-to-active-volunteer conversion (people who joined WhatsApp groups vs. people who sent the peer-ask to ≥5 contacts) | Measures the health of the ground game; a roster of inactive volunteers is not a ground game | Volunteer coordinator tracking |
| Organic shares of beneficiary story content (not likes — shares, especially into private/dark social channels) | Dark social shares (WhatsApp forwarding) are where persuasion happens; platform shares are the proxy | Platform native analytics (shares, not likes) |
| Earned media placements that mention a named beneficiary or the investigative finding (not just the NGO name) | Third-party credibility placements with specific content are trust signals; NGO name mentions alone are not | Manual tracking by Campaigner 2 |
| Press response rate to investigative pitch (replies vs. pitches sent) | Measures the newsworthiness of the investigation before publication | Campaigner 1 outreach log |

**Ignore these:**

| Metric | Why to ignore it |
|---|---|
| Total impressions | Impressions measure exposure, not persuasion. The [opposition] has far more impressions; this is not the metric you can win on. |
| Follower count (Instagram, Facebook Page) | Vanity. The NGO's 2,400 Instagram followers are not what converts. The email list of 8,000 is, and even that needs re-engagement. |
| Facebook Page engagement rate | Near 0.15% organically in 2026. Watching this number is watching a metric that cannot move meaningfully. |
| Platform-reported ROAS (if any paid is activated) | Attributed, not incremental. See §10 for why. The platforms' own Conversion Lift tools acknowledge this gap; the dashboard ROAS does not. |
| Raw likes on any content | Cheap, bot-inflatable, and do not correlate with petition conversion. Only shares and comments that continue the thread indicate real engagement. |
| Total email list size | Size without engagement is a vanity metric. A 600-person engaged list converting at 5% outperforms an 8,000-person disengaged list converting at 0.3%. Track open rate and click-to-conversion, not list size. |
| Social sentiment | Noisy, slow, and correlates poorly with the actual outcome (petition signatures, donations). Directionally interesting; not a decision input. |

---

**Sector failure mode to hold as a standing warning (from Rider 3):**

The most common NGO campaign failure is donor-speak instead of story. Every piece of content produced in this campaign should pass the following test before publication:

- Does it lead with one named person, one specific moment, one concrete ask?
- Is the first paragraph free of organizational jargon, acronyms, and output metrics ("we delivered X program hours")?
- Would a climate-skeptical but intellectually honest adult read this and feel spoken to, or spoken at?

If the answer to any of these is no, rewrite before publishing.

---

*Confirm or adjust any element above. The most consequential decisions for your specific situation are: (1) whether to redirect the €4,800 paid earmark as proposed; (2) whether the investigative journalism co-publication can be confirmed by end of week 1; and (3) whether beneficiary families can be recruited in weeks 1–2. If any of these three fail, the campaign shape shifts — return to §7 and route to the shape that does not depend on the failing element.*
